## Experiment Design: Multimodal Neural Network vs. Logistic Regression for Post-Operative Complication Prediction

### 1. Data Selection and Preparation

We would use electronic health records from a single hospital system spanning 5 years (60 months). The dataset comprises all patients undergoing orthopedic surgery with complete structured data (age, sex, BMI, ASA grade, preoperative labs including hemoglobin, albumin, creatinine), unstructured clinical text (preoperative assessment notes, operative reports, intraoperative notes), and verified post-operative outcomes. We would extract all patients with documented complications (surgical site infection, VTE, pneumonia, acute kidney injury, readmission, or return to OR) within 90 days post-surgery and balance these against uncomplicated cases.

The label is defined a priori using institutional complication criteria and validated against ICD-10 codes, with clinical adjudication by a physician blinded to model predictions to ensure consistency.

### 2. Train-Validation-Test Split Strategy

We would employ a temporal split with stratification. Months 1–36 are designated for training; months 37–48 for validation; months 49–60 for held-out test evaluation. This temporal strategy prevents information leakage and ensures the model generalizes to genuinely future patients, reflecting real-world deployment where clinical practice, protocols, and patient populations evolve.

Within each temporal window, we would stratify by surgeon (or surgical team) and complication rate quartile to ensure that surgical practice variability and case complexity are balanced across splits. This stratification mitigates confounding from individual surgeon technique, care protocols, and case selection that would otherwise correlate with outcomes.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Structured features include demographics (age, sex, BMI, ASA score), preoperative laboratory values (hemoglobin, albumin, creatinine, comorbidity burden), surgery type (one-hot encoded), and surgeon identifier (fixed effect). Unstructured clinical notes (preoperative and intraoperative) are embedded using a pretrained clinical language model (e.g., SciBERT or BioBERT fine-tuned on MIMIC) to generate fixed-length contextual vector representations. Derived features include days from admission to surgery.

All preprocessing—scaling of labs, imputation of missing values, text vectorization, and dimensionality reduction—is fit exclusively on the training set and applied identically to validation and test data. No statistics or learned parameters from validation or test sets inform preprocessing, eliminating leakage.

### 4. Model Architecture and Baseline

The neural network employs a multimodal architecture: a structured data branch (2–3 fully connected layers with batch normalization and dropout) processes demographics and labs; a text branch (frozen pretrained embeddings → pooling → fully connected layers) processes clinical notes. Branches are concatenated and passed through additional fully connected layers, culminating in a sigmoid output.

The baseline is logistic regression trained on the same structured feature set (demographics, labs, surgery type, surgeon effects) using identical preprocessing and equivalent tuning effort. Critically, the baseline does not incorporate text embeddings. This design isolates the hypothesis: the improvement comes from multimodal integration and neural capacity, not from additional features.

### 5. Hyperparameter Tuning and Model Selection

Both the neural network and logistic regression undergo hyperparameter optimization on the validation set (months 37–48). For the neural network, we tune layer sizes, dropout rates, learning rate, batch size, and optimization algorithm. For logistic regression, we optimize L1/L2 regularization strength. Model selection uses the primary metric (sensitivity at 85% fixed specificity) evaluated on validation data. The best model is selected and locked before test evaluation.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is sensitivity (recall) at a fixed specificity of 85%. This reflects clinical priorities: identifying at-risk patients for preventive intervention is the priority. A sensitivity floor ensures broad capture of complications while maintaining operational feasibility (clinician workload). Secondary metrics include AUROC, AUPRC (appropriate under potential class imbalance), calibration curves, and F1 score.

### 7. Test Set Evaluation and Reporting

The test set (months 49–60) is evaluated exactly once, at the conclusion of the experiment. Final performance is reported with 95% confidence intervals computed via bootstrap. Subgroup analysis stratifies test results by surgeon, complication severity, age, and surgery type to detect performance heterogeneity and identify generalization limitations.

### 8. Scope and Limitations

This design establishes whether the multimodal neural network achieves superior discrimination and sensitivity compared to logistic regression on structured data alone, within a single hospital system over a 5-year window. It does not establish whether the model is ready for clinical deployment (external validation on other hospitals, prospective validation, and cost-benefit analysis are required). Surgeon and practice-level confounding is mitigated through stratification and fixed effects modeling, but residual unmeasured confounding from individual surgeon skill cannot be eliminated.

hypothesis: Utilizing a recurrent neural network (RNN) to model patient symptom progression will achieve better accuracy in predicting disease severity at the next clinical visit compared to traditional logistic regression using static medical features.
task_prompt:
To test whether an RNN model predicts disease severity more accurately than logistic regression, we propose the following controlled experiment using the available 5-year EHR dataset.

**1. Data Preparation and Quality Assurance**

We will work with the full cohort of 10,000 patients and their temporal symptom, lab, and clinical note records spanning 60 months. Before proceeding, we must audit data quality: (1) Quantify the proportion and pattern of missing symptom and lab values; (2) Examine the distribution of visit intervals (median, range, skew) to understand the irregularity challenge; (3) Assess inter-rater reliability of clinician severity assessments by examining a stratified sample of patients with multiple assessments by different clinicians (target ICC ≥ 0.7; if lower, restrict to high-agreement clinicians or use consensus labels); (4) Document variability in treatment adherence documentation, noting which patients have complete records versus sparse adherence data.

**2. Data Splitting Strategy**

Temporality is critical: we split chronologically to prevent future information from leaking into training. The dataset is divided into three consecutive 20-month windows: months 1–36 form the training set (60%, ~6,000 patients with complete trajectories in this window), months 37–48 form the validation set (20%, ~2,000 patients), and months 49–60 form the held-out test set (20%, ~2,000 patients). Within each window, we stratify patients by baseline disease severity (quartile 1–4) and comorbidity burden (Charlson index: 0, 1–2, ≥3) to ensure all three splits have balanced representation of disease subtypes. This stratification controls for confounding by disease severity and comorbidity, and ensures the test set mirrors the demographic and clinical structure of the training population.

**3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing**

All preprocessing steps are fit on the training set and applied identically to validation and test.

*RNN features:* We construct temporal sequences per patient, ordered chronologically. Each sequence includes the symptom severity, key lab result values, and vital signs at each visit. We standardize sequence length to 24 visits (approximately 2 years of history; patients with fewer visits are zero-padded; those with more are truncated from oldest records). We compute two temporal metadata features: (a) days elapsed since the prior visit (normalized by z-score, using training set mean and SD), and (b) visit sequence index (0–23). We add static baseline features: age, sex, primary diagnosis code (one-hot encoded), and Charlson comorbidity index. If clinical notes are used, we extract a bag-of-words vector or apply a pre-trained clinical text embedding (fit vocabulary on training notes only).

*Baseline (logistic regression) features:* Only static features—age, sex, diagnosis, Charlson index, and the severity score from the most recent visit. No temporal dynamics are available to the baseline.

*Missing data handling:* Within-patient missing symptom values are forward-filled using the median forward-fill imputation strategy learned from the training set. Missing lab results are imputed using the training set median (computed per lab type). Any patient-visit pair still lacking a severity label (target variable) is excluded from analysis.

**4. Model Architecture and Baseline**

The RNN is a bidirectional LSTM (Bi-LSTM) with two stacked layers (64 hidden units per layer), dropout (0.3), and an output layer predicting continuous severity. Bidirectionality captures past and longitudinal context; temporal metadata features help the model handle irregular intervals. The baseline is logistic regression (or linear regression, if the severity scale is truly continuous) trained on static features only. Both models are given equal effort in hyperparameter tuning: grid search over learning rate (1e-3, 1e-2, 1e-1), batch size (16, 32, 64), dropout (0.2, 0.3, 0.4), and sequence length (12, 18, 24). Tuning is performed on the validation set; the configuration minimizing validation MAE is selected for both models.

**5. Evaluation Strategy**

*Model selection:* Hyperparameter tuning uses only the validation set (months 37–48). The model and baseline configurations with the lowest validation MAE are frozen. The test set is not examined during tuning.

*Primary metric:* Mean Absolute Error (MAE) on the test set, reported in the original severity score units. MAE is clinically interpretable (e.g., an MAE of 3 means predictions are off by 3 points on average) and aligns with the stakeholder's need for accurate point estimates of severity.

*Secondary metrics:* (1) Median Absolute Error (MdAE), for robustness; (2) RMSE, if clinicians incur nonlinear costs for larger errors; (3) R² on test set, contextualizing the variance explained; (4) MAE stratified by baseline severity quartile and comorbidity group, detecting whether the RNN's advantage is consistent across patient subgroups; (5) Calibration plots comparing predicted to observed severity within decile bins, ensuring predictions are trustworthy for clinical decision-making.

**6. Confound and Bias Controls**

*Treatment adherence:* Patients with different adherence levels may have different symptom trajectories. We control for this via stratified splitting (ensuring balanced comorbidity and baseline severity across splits, both proxies for adherence). If adherence data is available in the EHR, we include it as a feature in both models, converting it from a confound to a covariate.

*Clinician assessment variability:* We measure inter-rater reliability (ICC) on a sample of patients with multiple clinician assessments. If ICC < 0.7, we restrict analyses to high-agreement clinicians or use consensus labels. This reduces label noise.

*Temporal validity:* Our split ensures no future information contaminates training. Validation and test sets consist of visits strictly after the training window, preserving causal ordering.

*Generalization scope:* This experiment validates performance on the local hospital's population during the 5-year period. Generalization to other hospitals or future years would require external validation on a separate prospective cohort.

**7. Interpretation and Claims**

If the RNN's test MAE is statistically significantly lower than the baseline's (bootstrapped 95% CI), we can conclude that **temporal modeling of symptom progression improves severity prediction accuracy on this dataset**. This result would establish that the RNN captures clinically relevant dynamics not captured by static features. However, this result is specific to the observed population and hospital system; external generalization requires prospective validation.

hypothesis: A recurrent neural network (RNN) model utilizing historical sensor data predicts equipment failure events more accurately than a traditional statistical method for industrial machinery within a manufacturing plant.
domain: Manufacturing / industrial predictive maintenance for machinery
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: RNN vs. Statistical Baseline for Equipment Failure Prediction

### 1. Data Collection and Preparation

The experiment uses 36 months of sensor data from the plant's SCADA system, spanning all industrial machinery in the facility. Raw features include temperature, pressure, and vibration readings captured at their native sampling frequency. Ground truth labels are derived from the maintenance logs: a failure event is any unplanned corrective maintenance action with a confirmed timestamp. All records are cross-checked for data quality; entries with missing or conflicting timestamps are excluded during curation.

To provide actionable lead time and align with the plant's maintenance scheduling cycle, a failure event is operationalized as follows: sensor sequences occurring within 168 hours (7 days) before a logged failure are labeled positive (failure imminent); all other sequences are labeled negative (normal operation). This 7-day window is the primary parameter defining the prediction task.

### 2. Split Strategy

A temporal split stratified by machine type is used:

- **Training set:** Months 1–24 (24 months of data)
- **Validation set:** Months 25–30 (6 months of data)
- **Test set:** Months 31–36 (6 months of data)

**Rationale for temporal split:** Sensor sequences are inherently time-ordered; a temporal split prevents the model from accessing future failure information during training. This mirrors real-world deployment where the model makes predictions on unseen future time periods.

**Rationale for stratification by machine type:** Machines exhibit heterogeneous failure modes and sensor distributions. Stratifying ensures that each machine type appears proportionally in all three sets, preventing a scenario where rare machine types are absent from validation/test. This enables per-machine-type evaluation to detect whether the RNN generalizes equally well across the equipment portfolio.

### 3. Feature Engineering

All features are engineered from the training set only; fitted statistics are then applied to validation and test sets without refitting.

**Raw features:** Temperature, pressure, vibration (3 sensors).

**Engineered features (9 total):**
- Rolling mean, std, min, and max over 1-hour windows (4 statistics × 3 sensors = 12 features)
- Rolling mean, std, min, and max over 24-hour windows (4 × 3 = 12 features)
- First-order differences (rate of change) for each sensor (3 features)
- Operating state indicator (derived from sensor combinations to flag idle, active, or overload states)

**Sequence construction:** For the RNN, a 168-hour (7-day) lookback window is constructed for each sequence, providing temporal context that aligns with the 7-day pre-failure label window. The sequence is represented as a tensor of shape (168 timesteps, 9 features). For the baseline (logistic regression), the same 9 engineered features are averaged or summarized over the 168-hour window to produce a fixed-length feature vector (9 features).

No features are constructed from future information (e.g., maintenance schedule dates are excluded) or directly encode the label.

### 4. Model Architecture and Baseline

**RNN Model:** A bidirectional LSTM with two hidden layers (128 units each), dropout (0.3 per layer), and sigmoid output for binary classification. Training uses the Adam optimizer with binary cross-entropy loss and early stopping (patience: 10 epochs) on validation loss.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression trained on the same 9 engineered features (flattened, no sequence dimension). This represents the traditional statistical approach mentioned in the hypothesis.

**Justification:** Both models receive equivalent tuning effort. Hyperparameters for both are tuned via grid search on the validation set over the same ranges (e.g., regularization from 1e-5 to 1e-3, learning rate from 0.0001 to 0.001 for RNN). By using identical feature engineering and tuning methodology, the comparison isolates the contribution of the RNN's sequential modeling capacity from feature quality differences.

### 5. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation set (months 25–30) using grid search. The metric optimized is F1 score. The model and hyperparameter combination achieving the highest validation F1 is selected. The test set is never used during this process.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** F1 score. Rationale: the plant manager seeks both high recall (detecting failures to prevent unplanned downtime) and high precision (avoiding false alarms that trigger unnecessary maintenance). F1 balances these objectives and is robust to class imbalance, which is expected in failure prediction tasks.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Precision: quantifies the cost of false alarms (unnecessary maintenance).
- Recall: quantifies the risk of missed failures (unplanned downtime).
- Area Under ROC Curve (AUC): overall discriminative ability across decision thresholds.
- Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC): handles class imbalance and provides a single summary statistic.
- Per-machine-type F1: stratified evaluation to detect whether the RNN generalizes uniformly across equipment classes or exhibits degradation on specific machine types.

### 7. Test Set Evaluation and Controls

The test set (months 31–36) is held out and untouched during development. After model selection, both the RNN and baseline are evaluated on the test set exactly once. Results are reported with 95% confidence intervals derived from bootstrap resampling (1000 iterations).

To account for confounding operational changes (workload, maintenance practices), temporal stratification is used: changes within the training window are absorbed by model learning; sudden shifts in months 31–36 would degrade both models and would be detected as a test performance drop, signaling distribution shift. Per-machine-type evaluation detects any machine-specific performance degradation. Sensor diagnostics are reviewed for months 31–36 to flag any recalibration or replacement events that could confound results.

### 8. Scope of Claims

This design establishes that the RNN achieves higher F1 than logistic regression under the tested operational conditions, machine portfolio, and failure definition. It does not establish causality or generalization to new machine types or radically different operational regimes (e.g., seasonal production shifts not captured in the 36-month window).

hypothesis: Using a hierarchical attention mechanism in a neural network model outperforms a traditional RNN model for predicting patient readmission risk within 30 days of discharge for heart failure patients in a hospital setting.
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Hierarchical Attention vs. RNN for 30-Day Readmission Prediction

**Objective:** Test whether a hierarchical attention neural network outperforms a standard LSTM-based RNN in predicting 30-day hospital readmission risk for heart failure patients using EHR data.

### 1. Data Acquisition and Labeling

We will use electronic health records from a single hospital system spanning 5 years, comprising 10,000+ discharged heart failure patients. The dataset includes all clinical observations during the admission period (vitals, labs, medications, notes) and discharge records. The outcome label is binary: readmission within 30 days post-discharge (yes/no), determined by querying the EHR for any unplanned admission with an admission date ≤30 days after the index discharge. Patients with incomplete discharge records or lost to follow-up are excluded. Expected class imbalance (~20–30% readmitted) is acknowledged and addressed in the evaluation strategy.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

To respect the temporal nature of the problem and ensure the test set represents genuinely future data, we employ a chronological split:
- **Training set:** Years 1–3 of discharge records
- **Validation set:** Year 4 of discharge records  
- **Test set:** Year 5 of discharge records

This temporal design prevents the model from learning non-causal temporal associations and ensures that validation and test performance reflect realistic deployment scenarios where the model encounters data from a future time period. No random shuffling or stratification across time boundaries is applied.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Features are extracted at the time of discharge or within 24 hours prior. The feature set includes: static demographics (age, sex, race, insurance type); clinical history (prior readmissions, prior ICU admissions, comorbidity indices); admission characteristics (length of stay, admission source, discharge disposition); physiological measurements at discharge (ejection fraction, BNP/NT-proBNP, hemoglobin, creatinine, systolic BP); discharge medications (ACE inhibitors, beta-blockers, diuretics as binary flags); and temporal features (day of week, seasonality). Sequences of clinical observations during the admission period (e.g., daily vitals and labs) are retained as time-series inputs for the neural models.

All preprocessing transformers (scaling, categorical encoding, missing value imputation) are fit on the training set only and applied identically to validation and test sets. This prevents leakage of validation and test distributions into preprocessing parameters.

### 4. Model Specifications

**Proposed model (Hierarchical Attention RNN):** A neural network with hierarchical attention mechanisms: a multi-head attention layer processes temporal sequences of clinical observations (vitals, labs over admission), a secondary attention mechanism aggregates information across clinical domains, and a fully connected classification head produces a binary readmission prediction. Architecture details: embedding layer → hierarchical attention blocks (with residual connections) → 2–3 dense layers (ReLU activation, dropout) → sigmoid output.

**Baseline model (LSTM RNN):** A standard 2-layer LSTM with 128 hidden units, processing the same temporal sequences, with identical input features and output task. The LSTM baseline represents the current standard for temporal EHR modeling.

Both models process sequences of variable length (padded to the maximum admission length observed in the training set). Both are optimized with Adam and trained for up to 200 epochs with early stopping on validation AUC.

### 5. Model Selection and Hyperparameter Tuning

Hyperparameter search is performed on both models using the validation set (Year 4) with AUC as the selection metric. Search space includes learning rate, dropout, number of layers, and hidden dimensions. The best performing model checkpoint (maximum validation AUC) is selected for each approach. The test set is not accessed during this phase.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics and Performance Assessment

**Primary metric:** Area under the ROC curve (AUC). AUC is chosen because it is threshold-agnostic, robust to class imbalance, and directly reflects the operational objective: ranking high-risk readmission patients above low-risk patients for care management intervention.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC): More sensitive to the minority class and useful for understanding clinical sensitivity.
- Sensitivity at 90% specificity: Operational proxy for detection rate at acceptable false positive rate.
- Brier score and calibration plots: Ensure predicted probabilities are reliable for risk stratification.
- Stratified performance by insurance type, age group, and discharge disposition: Detect whether improvements are uniform or concentrated in subgroups.

Final evaluation is performed once on the held-out test set (Year 5). Test results are reported for both models, and the difference in AUC (hierarchical attention – baseline LSTM) is the primary quantitative outcome.

### 7. Confound Control and Sensitivity Analysis

Known confounds (hospital protocols, insurance, referral patterns) are addressed by: (1) retaining confound variables as features in both models; (2) evaluating performance stratified by key confound categories to detect heterogeneous effects; (3) examining feature distributions across train/val/test sets to detect distribution shifts indicating protocol changes; and (4) reporting results from a single hospital system, with acknowledgment that multi-site validation is required for generalization claims.

### 8. Success Criteria

The hypothesis is supported if the hierarchical attention model achieves higher test-set AUC than the LSTM baseline, with the improvement being statistically meaningful (e.g., ≥0.03 absolute AUC difference) and consistent across secondary metrics and subgroups. If improvement is limited to specific subgroups, the claim is narrowed accordingly.

hypothesis: A convolutional neural network (CNN) using multi-spectral imaging data outperforms a traditional RGB-based model in predicting the biomass of algal blooms in freshwater lakes during summer months.
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Multi-Spectral CNN for Algal Biomass Prediction

### 1. Data Strategy

The experiment uses satellite multi-spectral imagery acquired over 5 years (June–August, summer months) from 8–12 well-characterized freshwater lakes known to experience seasonal algal blooms. Images are paired with in-situ biomass measurements (chlorophyll-a concentration or dry biomass) collected via standard limnological sampling within ±2 days of image acquisition. Data is sourced from USGS/NASA satellite archives or equivalent environmental monitoring networks, yielding an expected dataset of 3,000–5,000 image–label pairs. 

Labels are defined as continuous biomass values measured by rigorous in-situ sampling protocols (HPLC, gravimetric analysis). Any measurement flagged as non-standard, delayed (>2 days), or taken from depths >2 m is excluded to maintain label quality. This approach ensures the label distribution matches the operational target: summer algal bloom monitoring in freshwater systems where stakeholders require accurate continuous biomass predictions to inform water quality management and public health decisions.

### 2. Temporal and Stratified Split Strategy

To prevent temporal leakage and control for site-specific biases, the data is split **by year and water body**. The split is:
- **Train:** Years 1–3, all lakes (60% of pairs)
- **Validation:** Year 4, all lakes (20% of pairs)
- **Test:** Year 5, all lakes (20% of pairs)

Within each year–lake partition, image–label pairs are randomly shuffled. This temporal split prevents future information (year 5 data) from influencing training, ensures the models generalize to unseen years, and reflects the operational scenario where a model trained on historical data must predict future blooms. The by-lake stratification ensures both models see the same distribution of lake types (oligotrophic, eutrophic, different water clarity) during training and validation, eliminating site-specific differences as a source of bias in the comparison.

### 3. Preprocessing and Feature Strategy

All preprocessing transformers (radiometric calibration, atmospheric correction, normalization) are **fit only on the training set** and applied identically to validation and test sets. This prevents leakage of information from validation/test data into preprocessing parameters.

The **CNN model** ingests 10 multi-spectral bands (e.g., blue, green, red, near-infrared, shortwave-infrared, plus coastal aerosol if available) as stacked tensors, normalized per-band using z-score or percentile scaling fitted on training data. Spatial patches are 64×64 or 128×128 pixels centered on the sampling location or largest bloom region.

The **RGB baseline** uses only red, green, and blue channels, extracted from the same multi-spectral product, matched to identical spatial patches and resolutions. Both models receive the same radiometric preprocessing; the comparison isolates the benefit of additional spectral information, not preprocessing choices. No hand-crafted spectral indices (NDVI, NDCI, etc.) are computed a priori; the CNN learns spectral relationships from raw bands.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The **multi-spectral CNN** consists of 4–6 convolutional blocks (32–256 filters, 3×3 kernels, ReLU), batch normalization, max-pooling (2×2), dropout (p=0.3–0.5), global average pooling, and 2–3 fully connected layers (128–256 units) with a linear regression output. Loss function: MSE or Huber loss. Optimizer: Adam with learning-rate decay. Training: early stopping on validation MAE, max 200 epochs, batch size 32.

The **RGB-only baseline** uses an identical architecture and training procedure, varying only the input channels (3 instead of 10). Both models undergo equivalent hyperparameter search: grid or random search over learning rate (1e-4 to 1e-2), dropout (0.2–0.5), filter counts, and regularization. Both are tuned on the validation set. This fair comparison isolates the benefit of multi-spectral information.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Mean Absolute Error (MAE) on the test set. MAE is chosen because it directly reflects operational constraints: environmental agencies require predictions within a specific tolerance (e.g., ±5 µg/L) for decision-making, and MAE measures exactly this. It is also robust to outliers, which is critical for extreme bloom events.

**Secondary metrics:**
- RMSE (sensitivity to large errors)
- R² (explained variance)
- Per-quantile calibration (residuals for low, medium, high biomass regimes to detect bias in different bloom intensities)
- Per-lake test MAE (generalization across water body types)

Statistical significance of the MAE difference is assessed via paired bootstrap resampling (1,000 replicates) on held-out test images, with 95% confidence intervals reported.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the **validation set (year 4)**. For each candidate configuration, validation MAE is computed. The configuration yielding the lowest validation MAE is selected for the CNN and separately for the RGB baseline.

The **test set (year 5) is sealed** and not accessed during tuning. After model selection is finalized, the test set is evaluated exactly once for each model, and results are reported with confidence intervals.

### 7. Controls for Confounds

Water clarity (Secchi depth, turbidity) and nutrient levels (nitrogen, phosphorus) are measured concurrently. Confound control is achieved through:

1. **Stratified test evaluation:** Performance is separately reported for high-clarity vs. low-clarity and high-nutrient vs. low-nutrient subsets, revealing whether the multi-spectral advantage is robust or restricted to specific conditions.

2. **Residual analysis:** Prediction residuals are regressed against water-clarity and nutrient covariates to detect systematic bias or model-specific sensitivity.

3. **Sensitivity subset:** The experiment is re-run on a subset of lakes with matched nutrient levels, isolating nutrient confounding.

These steps do not eliminate confounding but transparently expose generalization boundaries: stakeholders learn whether multi-spectral improvements hold across diverse lake environments or apply only under specific conditions.

### Scope and Claims

This design establishes that, in summer months on freshwater lakes in the observed region, multi-spectral CNN predictions have lower test-set MAE than RGB-only predictions under identical training and tuning effort. It does **not** establish causality, seasonal robustness outside summer, or generalization to unmeasured regions or lake types. The by-lake stratification and per-lake secondary metrics clarify these boundaries.

hypothesis: A recurrent neural network (RNN) model using historical patient visit sequences predicts hospital readmission rates better than a logistic regression model using standard demographic and clinical features.
domain: Healthcare - Predicting hospital readmissions from electronic health records
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: RNN vs. Logistic Regression for Hospital Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data and Labels

This experiment uses electronic health records from a single healthcare system spanning 36 months of patient encounters. The dataset contains approximately 200,000 visits with complete demographic, clinical, diagnostic, and outcome information. The outcome is defined prospectively: a patient is labeled positive for readmission if they return to any hospital in the system within 30 days of discharge from the index visit. This label is determined from institutional admission records and is not subject to missing data.

### 2. Data Split Strategy

To preserve temporal integrity and prevent leakage, we employ a temporal split: training data spans months 1–24 (66%), validation data covers months 25–30 (17%), and the held-out test set covers months 31–36 (17%). Within each split, we stratify by admission urgency (emergency vs. planned/urgent) and Charlson Comorbidity Index tertiles. This stratification ensures both models are evaluated on clinically representative populations with balanced acuity distributions across all three sets. The temporal structure is critical: the RNN will exploit sequential patterns in visit history, and a random split would allow it to observe future visit information relative to the training window, creating an unfair advantage. The stratified approach controls for baseline acuity, a known confound influencing readmission risk independent of model architecture.

### 3. Feature Engineering

**Logistic regression features** (baseline): age (years), sex, race/ethnicity, Charlson Comorbidity Index score, presence of ≥3 and ≥5 chronic diagnoses, insurance type, number of hospitalizations in the prior 12 months, current length of stay, and primary diagnosis category (ICD-10 grouping). All features use raw or standardly aggregated EHR values with no lookahead.

**RNN features** (treatment): for each patient, we construct a sequence of up to 50 prior visits within the 12 months preceding the index visit, in chronological order. Each visit is encoded as a fixed-length vector containing: (1) days since the prior visit (binned), (2) primary diagnosis (one-hot encoded from the 100 most frequent ICD-10 codes; others pooled as 'other'), (3) admission type (categorical: emergency/urgent/planned), (4) length of stay (continuous), (5) aggregated procedure counts by category. Sequences shorter than 50 visits are left-padded with zeros. This representation preserves temporal order and visit-level clinical detail.

**Preprocessing fit point**: All transformers—including standardization of continuous variables, categorical encoders, and sequence tokenization—are fit on the training set only. Parameters (mean, std, vocabulary) derived from training data are then applied identically to validation and test data. This prevents any leakage of test or validation information into preprocessing.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Treatment model**: A bidirectional LSTM with 2 stacked layers (128 hidden units each), dropout of 0.3 between layers, trained with Adam optimizer and binary cross-entropy loss. This architecture is standard for sequential clinical data and captures both forward and backward dependencies in visit sequences.

**Baseline model**: Logistic regression with L2 regularization (C parameter tuned via 5-fold cross-validation nested within the training set on validation recall). The baseline receives identical preprocessing effort and tuning intensity as the LSTM. Logistic regression represents the current clinical standard for readmission prediction and provides a fair, interpretable comparison.

### 5. Validation and Model Selection

The validation set (months 25–30) is used to: (1) select hyperparameters (regularization strength for logistic regression via nested CV; early stopping epoch and decision threshold for the LSTM), and (2) determine the decision threshold for both models such that predicted positive cases achieve 80% precision. The validation set is never used for final evaluation.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric**: Recall at 80% precision. This aligns with the stakeholder objective: maximizing detection of readmission-risk patients while maintaining clinical actionability (at least 80% of flagged patients are true positives). Recall quantifies sensitivity—how many patients who will truly be readmitted are identified.

**Secondary metrics**: (1) AUROC to assess discrimination across all thresholds, (2) AUPRC to account for class imbalance, (3) F1-score at the 80% precision threshold, (4) Expected Calibration Error (ECE) in decile bins to ensure predicted probabilities match observed readmission rates.

### 7. Test Set Evaluation and Final Reporting

The test set (months 31–36) is evaluated exactly once after all tuning is complete. Both models are applied with fixed parameters derived from training and validation phases. Primary and secondary metrics are computed on the test set, and performance is stratified by age group, comorbidity burden, and diagnosis category to check for consistency and detect subgroup differences. Patients with fewer than 5 prior visits in the 12-month window are analyzed separately to ensure the RNN does not rely on high-volume patient artifacts.

### 8. Scope and Confounds

This design establishes whether the RNN achieves higher recall than logistic regression at 80% precision on held-out test data and whether this advantage is robust across clinically meaningful subgroups. Stratification by comorbidity and urgency controls for baseline acuity as a confound. Post-hoc subgroup analysis detects any performance heterogeneity. The design does not establish generalizability to other health systems, causality, or the optimality of the 30-day readmission window.

hypothesis: A convolutional neural network (CNN) trained on high-resolution CT scans of lung images detects early-stage lung cancer with higher sensitivity than traditional radiologist assessments for a diverse cohort of patients.
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: CNN Sensitivity for Early-Stage Lung Cancer Detection

### 1. Data and Labeling

We use all 10,000 CT scan images from the hospital archive. To establish a reliable gold standard, we define the label through consensus: each image is independently assessed by two radiologists with ≥5 years of experience, using structured criteria (Lung-RADS v1.1 or equivalent). Cases of disagreement are resolved by a third expert radiologist, blind to the initial assessments. We confirm the label with histopathology (biopsy/surgery) or clinical follow-up of ≥24 months for negative-labeled cases. This multi-rater approach reduces the inter-rater variability confound by establishing a robust ground truth that is not itself a single radiologist's opinion.

### 2. Data Splits

We employ a stratified split that preserves both cancer prevalence and institution representation. Images are divided into 70% training (7,000 images), 15% validation (1,500 images), and 15% test (1,500 images). Critically, no images from a single patient appear in multiple splits. Stratification by institution and disease prevalence ensures that each split reflects the demographic and technical diversity of the full dataset, supporting generalization to new patients from different hospital sites. The test set is locked before any model training begins.

### 3. Preprocessing and Augmentation

Preprocessing transformers (intensity normalization to Hounsfield units, bias field correction, spatial registration if applicable) are fit on the training set alone. The fitted parameters are applied identically to validation and test sets to prevent data leakage. During training, we apply data augmentation (random rotations, elastic deformations, intensity jittering) to the training batch on-the-fly. Augmentation is not applied to validation or test data, as these are held fixed for reproducible evaluation.

### 4. Features and Model

The model operates on raw CT voxel intensities, allowing the CNN to learn hierarchical feature representations end-to-end. We do not hand-engineer features; instead, we evaluate several CNN architectures (ResNet-50 with ImageNet pretraining, a custom 3D volumetric CNN, or a Vision Transformer) on the validation set and select the best performer. Metadata (age, gender, smoking history) are held separate and not used in the primary model, isolating the hypothesis to image-based detection; metadata may be incorporated in a secondary multimodal model for robustness analysis. Hyperparameters (learning rate, weight decay, dropout, batch size) are tuned via grid or random search on the validation set.

### 5. Baseline and Fairness

The baseline is the sensitivity achieved by radiologist consensus on the same test set. We compute this as the fraction of test cases where both (or the majority of) radiologists correctly identified early-stage cancer. This baseline is operationally meaningful because it represents the current clinical standard. We do not 'tune' radiologists; their assessment is fixed. A secondary baseline uses simple Lung-RADS thresholds applied to radiologist measurements.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is **sensitivity** (true positive rate), calculated on the test set. We select the CNN's decision threshold on the validation set to match or exceed the radiologist consensus sensitivity, ensuring a fair operational comparison. The primary metric directly addresses the clinical objective: minimizing missed early-stage diagnoses. Secondary metrics include specificity, AUC-ROC, precision, F1-score, and calibration (expected calibration error). Critically, we compute per-subgroup sensitivity and specificity stratified by age, gender, smoking history, and scanner type to detect whether performance varies across demographic or technical groups and to surface potential confounds.

### 7. Confound Controls

Stratified splits preserve institution and disease prevalence, reducing dataset bias. Subgroup analysis reveals differential performance across populations. A sensitivity analysis excludes the highest-volume institution to verify the model does not overfit to one site's protocols. All metrics are reported with 95% confidence intervals via bootstrap or exact binomial methods. The test set is evaluated once only, after model selection is complete, preventing threshold selection bias.

### 8. Claims and Scope

This design will establish whether the CNN achieves higher sensitivity than radiologist consensus on a diverse, labeled test set. It will not establish clinical causation (e.g., whether using the CNN changes patient outcomes) nor will it guarantee performance on future institutional partners with different scanners or protocols, though the stratified approach mitigates this risk. The generalization claim is limited to the diversity present in the archive; external prospective validation would be required for clinical deployment.

hypothesis: Utilizing deep reinforcement learning to optimize adaptive traffic signal timings reduces vehicle wait times at intersection control points more effectively than traditional fixed-time signal management, as measured during peak traffic hours.
domain: Urban traffic management and control systems
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Deep Reinforcement Learning for Adaptive Traffic Signal Control

### 1. Objective and Scope
To test whether a Double DQN agent trained to optimize signal phase timings reduces average vehicle wait times more effectively than fixed-time and baseline adaptive signal control during peak traffic hours, we propose a temporal experiment spanning 18 months of real-world traffic data from 3–5 urban intersections. The hypothesis will be evaluated against two baselines: current fixed-time control and any existing adaptive system. Success is measured via average wait time (sec/vehicle) during peak hours (7–9 AM and 5–7 PM, weekdays only).

### 2. Data Collection and Preparation
We would use traffic monitoring data collected continuously from the selected intersections over 18 months: 12 months of baseline data (months 1–12, used for RL training) and 6 months of experimental data (months 13–18, split into validation and test). Raw data sources include inductive loop detectors (vehicle counts and occupancy per lane, 5-min aggregation) and signal controller logs (phase timings, phase transitions). Wait times are computed from detector occupancy data using standard queueing approximations (e.g., queue position relative to detector). Any missing data due to detector failures is flagged; gaps under 30 minutes are forward-filled; longer gaps are excluded from analysis. Total dataset should comprise at least 50 million vehicle observations across all sites.

### 3. Temporal Split Strategy and Leakage Prevention
A strict temporal split is critical for this hypothesis. The training period comprises months 1–12: the RL agent learns signal timings using historical traffic patterns from this window. The validation period comprises months 13–15: this period is used for hyperparameter tuning (network architecture, learning rate, exploration schedule) and is **never** touched during final evaluation. The test period comprises months 16–18: this is held completely out-of-sample and evaluated only once at the conclusion of training and validation. This sequential split respects the causal ordering of RL (the agent must learn before it can be deployed) and prevents future traffic information from influencing the learned policy.

Within each period, data is further stratified by day-of-week and time-of-day to ensure training covers diverse traffic regimes (e.g., weekday mornings, weekday evenings, weekend patterns). This stratified sampling ensures the learned policy generalizes across different traffic conditions within the training window.

### 4. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing
The RL agent's state representation includes: (1) current and prior 3 cycles of queue lengths per lane (normalized); (2) vehicle arrival rates over the last 15 minutes per lane; (3) cyclic time-of-day encoding (hour as sin/cos); (4) day-of-week; (5) detector occupancy; (6) elapsed signal phase duration as a fraction of total phase time. All continuous features are z-score normalized using mean and standard deviation **computed on the training period only**; these normalization parameters are fixed and applied without refitting to validation and test data. This prevents validation and test statistics from leaking into the feature space.

Missing detector values are handled before feature construction: forward-fill within 30-minute windows; longer gaps are excluded. Importantly, features are derived only from detector readings and signal logs—never from wait time labels or future observations—to ensure no label leakage.

### 5. Baseline Design
Two baselines are compared to the DQN agent:
- **Baseline 1 (Fixed-Time):** Current deployed signal timings (e.g., 60-sec cycle, fixed phase splits by direction). These are applied deterministically throughout the test period with no adaptation.
- **Baseline 2 (Adaptive, if available):** Existing adaptive signal control (e.g., vehicle-actuated or time-of-day adaptive logic currently in use).

Both baselines use the same input data and are evaluated under identical conditions (same test period, same metric computation). The DQN agent is given equivalent resources: hyperparameter tuning via grid search on validation data, computational budget for 100,000 simulated training cycles per intersection. Baselines receive no tuning—they reflect standard practice—ensuring fair comparison.

### 6. Model and Training
The model is a Deep Reinforcement Learning agent based on Double DQN (Dueling variant) to mitigate overestimation bias. The agent is trained in a deterministic traffic simulator initialized with statistical properties from the training data. Network architecture: 3-layer fully connected network (64–64–32 neurons, ReLU activations, dueling streams for value and advantage functions). Training hyperparameters: Adam optimizer (learning rate 0.001), epsilon-greedy exploration (ε decays from 0.3 to 0.05 over training), prioritized experience replay, target network update frequency every 1000 steps. The action space is discrete signal phase duration (60–120 sec in 10-sec increments). The reward signal is the negative average wait time in the cycle plus a small penalty for phase transitions (to discourage flickering).

### 7. Model Selection and Validation
Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the **validation period only** (months 13–15). We evaluate candidate hyperparameter sets by simulating the learned policy on validation traffic and measuring the resulting average wait time. Early stopping is applied: training halts if validation wait time does not improve for 5,000 simulated cycles. The best model (highest performance on validation) is selected. Importantly, validation data is used only for tuning; it is not part of the final test evaluation.

### 8. Evaluation on Held-Out Test Data
The final evaluation uses the test period (months 16–18) and is performed **exactly once**. No model retraining, threshold adjustment, or further tuning occurs on test data. The frozen DQN agent is applied to test traffic, and average wait time is computed for all cycles during peak hours. Results are reported with 95% confidence intervals computed via block bootstrap (weekly blocks) to account for temporal autocorrelation. Test results are disaggregated by intersection and by direction (northbound, southbound, etc.) to diagnose where improvements occur.

### 9. Primary and Secondary Metrics
**Primary Metric:** Average vehicle wait time (sec/vehicle) during peak hours in the test period, macro-averaged across intersections. This directly aligns with stakeholder objectives. Success is defined as statistically significant reduction (two-tailed t-test, α=0.05) relative to both baselines.

**Secondary Metrics:**
- 95th percentile wait time (tail congestion; captures user experience in worst-case scenarios)
- Throughput (vehicles/hour exiting each intersection) to ensure improved wait time does not reflect reduced traffic volume
- Phase transition frequency (to detect signal instability or excessive flickering)
- Wait time variance across lanes (fairness analysis; are improvements uniform or concentrated?)

### 10. Confound Control and Robustness
Several known confounds could bias results:

**Incidents (accidents, construction):** Traffic data are annotated with incident flags (via city traffic management logs or external APIs). Analysis is stratified: results are reported both overall and stratified by incident presence/absence. This isolates the effect of RL control from external shocks.

**Seasonal variation:** The test period (months 16–18) is aligned to the same calendar months in the baseline year (prior 18 months) where possible. If this is infeasible, seasonal indicators are included as features, and interaction effects are examined in post-hoc analysis.

**Intersection heterogeneity:** Results are disaggregated by intersection. If performance varies widely across sites (coefficient of variation > 30%), post-hoc stratification by intersection characteristics (volume, geometry, surrounding land use) is performed to identify factors driving heterogeneity.

**Policy changes:** Any policy implementations (speed limit changes, bus lane additions) during the test window are logged. Sensitivity analyses exclude affected periods from the final result.

### 11. Scope and Limitations
This experiment establishes that the DQN agent reduces wait time in the observed traffic regime during the test period at the selected intersections. It does **not** establish: (a) generalization to future years or different cities without retraining, (b) performance under novel traffic patterns absent from training data, (c) robustness to large-scale network effects (e.g., coordinated signal control across multiple intersections), or (d) safety or fairness properties (e.g., whether certain vehicle types or routes receive worse service). Future work should address these via separate studies.
